translatedText: When did Nzinga Mbande seize power in Ndongo?
Nzinga Mbande seized power over Ndongo after her brother Mbandi died in 1624.
What alliances did Nzinga Mbande form?
In search of allies, Nzinga Mbande married the Imbangala general Kasanje, and in 1641, she formed an alliance with the Dutch West India Company, which had captured Luanda from Portugal.
341 BC
Born on the island of Samos in Asia Minor
311 BC
Expelled from Mytilene due to backlash after his lectures
306 BC
Founded "The Garden" in Athens as a meeting place for his school
270 BC
Died of kidney stones
Founded Epicureanism
He founded the school of Epicureanism.
Presented the Purpose of Philosophy
He saw the purpose of philosophy as attaining a happy and tranquil life.
Founder of Hedonistic Philosophy
He became the founder of hedonistic philosophy by advocating atomistic materialism and ethical hedonism.
Established the Concepts of Ataraxia and Aponia
He presented peace of mind (ataraxia) and absence of pain (aponia) as characteristics of a happy and tranquil life.
Presented a Perspective on Death
He taught that death is the end of the body and soul, and therefore not something to be feared.
Presented a Perspective on Gods
He argued that the gods neither punish nor reward humans, and do not care about them.
Developed Atomistic Materialism
He explained that all phenomena in the world result from the movements and interactions of atoms moving through empty space.
Introduced the Concept of Free Will
By stating that atomic motion is not a simple straight line but occasionally swerves, he avoided determinism and allowed for free will.
Introduced the Idea of Human Equality
He introduced the fundamental concept of human equality to the Greeks by accepting women and slaves into his school.
Proposed the Principle of Multiple Explanations
He proposed the "Principle of Multiple Explanations," which contributed to the philosophy of science.
What is the core purpose of Epicurean philosophy?
For Epicurus, the purpose of philosophy was to attain a happy and tranquil life characterized by peace of mind (ataraxia), peace, freedom from fear, and the absence of pain (aponia).
What did Epicurus teach about death?
Epicurus taught that death is the end of the body and soul and is not to be feared; when we exist, death is not present, and when death is present, we do not exist, so death is nothing to us.
What were the characteristics of the Epicurean school?
The Epicurean school was the first to officially accept women, valued friendship as an important ingredient of happiness, and advocated for a secluded life rather than political participation.
What does pleasure mean according to Epicurus?
Epicurus viewed pleasure as the absence of pain and pursued lasting, static pleasure rather than momentary, physical pleasure.
Born on September 22 in Newington Butts as the third of four children to James and Margaret Faraday.
At age 13, became an apprentice to George Riebau, a local bookseller.
On August 7, signed a seven-year apprenticeship contract with George Riebau.
Read Jane Marcet's "Conversations on Chemistry" and the 3rd edition of the Encyclopædia Britannica edited by James Tytler.
Began reading articles on art and science in newspapers and magazines and taking notes.
Received tickets to Humphry Davy's chemistry lectures and attended them; wrote a letter to Davy asking for help.
Started working at the Royal Institution in March; began a European tour as Davy's secretary and assistant on October 13.
Traveled to Naples, Italy, climbed Mount Vesuvius, and met Alessandro Volta in Pavia.
Returned to England on April 23; in May, was rehired as an assistant and superintendent of apparatus and mineralogical collections at the Royal Institution laboratory.
Married Sarah Barnard on June 12; published a paper on electromagnetic rotation in the "Annals of Philosophy".
Left a note in his laboratory notebook saying, "Convert magnetism into electricity."
Succeeded in liquefying chlorine.
Discovered benzene; was commissioned by the Royal Society to study the optical properties of glass for use in telescopes.
Determined the molecular structure of naphthalene and synthesized two crystal samples; established the Christmas Lectures for children.
In April, published a paper containing a guide to practical chemistry; received a special furnace from the Royal Institution to begin optical glass experiments.
Announced the success of his optical glass research at the Bakerian Lecture of the Royal Society.
Began electromagnetic experiments on August 29 and discovered electromagnetic induction.
At the end of the year, began research on 'electrochemical decomposition' and discovered Faraday's first law of electrolysis.
At the end of the year, experimented with various substances dissolved in water using a voltameter and discovered Faraday's second law of electrolysis.
Discovered that the charge on a charged conductor resides only on its exterior surface.
Published the first volume of "Experimental Researches in Electricity".
Succeeded in liquefying many substances including ammonia and carbon dioxide.
Met William Thomson at Cambridge University in June; on September 18, defined the physical relationship between magnetism and light, discovering the Faraday effect.
Started experiments to investigate changes in diamagnetic materials under the influence of a magnet and mentioned the concept of a magnetic field.
At the request of Queen Victoria, was allowed to live in a house near Hampton Court along the River Thames in London.
Published "Experimental Researches in Chemistry and Physics".

Submitted research results on gravity and electricity to the "Philosophical Transactions" but was advised to withdraw it; delivered the lecture "The Chemical History of a Candle" at the Royal Institution's Christmas Lectures.
Symptoms of memory loss began; gave his final Christmas lecture.
Conducted experiments on whether the spectrum of sodium and other metals heated by a flame is affected by a magnetic field.
Passed away on August 25.
Discovery of Electromagnetic Induction
Discovered the principles of electromagnetic induction, diamagnetism, and the laws of electrolysis, and invented an electromagnetic rotary device that formed the foundation for the electric motor.
Discovery of Benzene and Introduction of Chemical Terms
As a chemist, he discovered benzene, investigated the clathrate hydrate of chlorine, and introduced technical terms such as anode, cathode, electrode, and ion for the first time.
Success in Gas Liquefaction
Successfully liquefied many substances including chlorine, ammonia, and carbon dioxide, demonstrating the existence of a critical point and the continuity of states of matter.
Research on Optical Glass Production
Researched the optical properties of glass for use in telescopes and developed a method to produce uniform glass free of bubbles or striae.
Discovery of the Faraday Effect
Established that a magnetic field could influence a light ray and the fundamental relationship between them, discovering the rotation of the plane of polarization in a magnetic field.
Classification of Magnetic Materials

Coined the terms diamagnetic for materials that align perpendicularly to a magnetic field and paramagnetic for those that align parallel, discovering that most gases are diamagnetic and oxygen is paramagnetic.
Introduction of the Magnetic Field Concept

Believed that magnetic lines of force had physical reality and was the first to mention the concept of a magnetic field where the influence received from two magnetic poles varies depending on position.
Discovery of the Faraday Cage Principle
Discovered that the charge on a charged conductor resides only on its exterior surface and has no influence on anything enclosed within it.
Establishment of the Christmas Lectures
To popularize science, he established the 'Christmas Lectures for children' in 1826, which has become a tradition of the Royal Institution in London.
What was Michael Faraday's educational background?

Faraday received very little formal or higher education in his childhood, learning only the basics of reading, writing, and arithmetic. He acquired his scientific knowledge primarily through self-study.
Was Faraday proficient in mathematics?

Faraday was only capable of simple algebra and lacked mathematical skills to the extent that he could not handle trigonometry, but he excelled in visual thinking and imagination. His theories were later mathematically reformulated by James Clerk Maxwell.
Did Faraday pursue fame or wealth?

No, he was not interested in worldly wealth; he refused to publish his lectures for profit and declined the presidency of the Royal Society as well as a knighthood. His humility and frugality stemmed from his religious beliefs.
Did Faraday have children?
No. Faraday was married to Sarah Barnard, but they had no children. However, he contributed to the popularization of science by creating and participating in science lectures for children.
How did Faraday's religion influence his scientific research?
Faraday was a devout member of the Sandemanian church, a fundamentalist Christian sect. His humility, frugality, and pure pursuit of knowledge are closely tied to this religious background.
November 8, 1028
Born in Falaise, Duchy of Normandy
January 1035
Received oaths of loyalty from Norman magnates as his father's successor
Early July 1035
Succeeded to the title of Duke of Normandy after the death of his father Robert I in Nicaea
Suppressed rebellions and began asserting authority over the duchy
Early 1047
Won the Battle of Val-ès-Dunes alongside King Henry I of France
Succeeded in driving out Guy of Burgundy
Early 1050s
Married Matilda of Flanders
King Edward of England seemingly chose William as his successor
February 1054
Defended against a two-pronged attack on Normandy by Henry and Norman rebels
Henry and Geoffrey Martel were defeated at the Battle of Varaville
Around 1060
Firmly secured the territory of Normandy as his own
